Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Pantothenate permease; major facilitator superfamily; predicted 12 transmembrane domains
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression increases intracellular pantothenate but does not raise CoA levels
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct value for CoA increase because transport is not rate-limiting; may improve pantothenate uptake only
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central acyl-group carrier in carbon metabolism, fatty acid metabolism, TCA cycle and biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. panF encodes the E. coli pantothenate permease mediating sodium-coupled pantothenate uptake. 2. PanF overexpression increases pantothenate uptake about 10-fold and raises intracellular pantothenate. 3. CoA levels remain unchanged, showing pantothenate uptake by PanF does not regulate CoA biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Pantothenate uptake is unidirectional; pantothenate efflux persists in panF mutants, implying a distinct unknown efflux system
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes pantothenate uptake but does not alter steady-state CoA level

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Hydrogen ion-pantothenate symporter; major facilitator superfamily
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: fen2 mutations confer resistance to fenpropimorph; loss impairs growth on minimal medium
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate pantothenate uptake in fungi, but not shown to directly control CoA pool
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Required under low pantothenate/minimal medium conditions
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low extracellular pantothenate; minimal medium; fenpropimorph exposure
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FEN2 encodes the yeast high-affinity pantothenate transporter. 2. Fen2p is essential for growth on minimal medium and mediates proton-coupled pantothenate uptake. 3. Mutations in FEN2 alter drug sensitivity, linking pantothenate transport with cellular physiology.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Primary sequence is unrelated to bacterial PanF and mammalian multivitamin transporter
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables pantothenate uptake needed to sustain CoA-related metabolism under low-vitamin conditions

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion causes slow growth and delayed septation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct application; relevant to fungal pantothenate utilization
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. liz1 encodes an active pantothenate transport protein in S. pombe. 2. Liz1 is required for uptake at low pantothenate concentrations. 3. liz1 deletion mutants remain viable but show slow growth and delayed septation, consistent with impaired CoA-dependent metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion mutant analysis; homology and uptake phenotype
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutants can synthesize some pantothenate from uracil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports pantothenate uptake and thereby CoA-linked metabolism under low-vitamin conditions

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter; sodium-coupled glucose transporter family; 13 transmembrane helices
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Shared transport with biotin and lipoate implies possible competition among substrates
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential nutritional target to increase pantothenate supply, but not shown to control CoA synthesis directly
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supplies pantothenate precursor for CoA biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Intestine, placenta, renal brush-border and other epithelial contexts described
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Homo sapiens; Rattus norvegicus; Oryctolagus cuniculus
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sodium gradient; competition by biotin and lipoate
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The mammalian sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter mediates active pantothenate uptake. 2. Cloned transporters confer sodium-dependent pantothenate transport with micromolar affinity. 3. The transporter also carries biotin and lipoate, indicating shared vitamin uptake capacity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous expression in mammalian cells/Xenopus oocytes; transport assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review discusses transport rather than direct CoA pool control
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes pantothenate uptake, supplying precursor for CoA biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Homo sapiens; Rattus norvegicus; Oryctolagus cuniculus

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss could impair removal of oxidized CoA species and alter peroxisomal beta-oxidation homeostasis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering node to tune CoA turnover, but direct physiological control remains untested
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: CoA supports fatty acid beta-oxidation; its turnover may maintain peroxisomal metabolic capacity
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Oxidative status; peroxisomal fatty acid beta-oxidation demand
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PCD1 encodes a peroxisomal CoA diphosphatase/nudix hydrolase. 2. The enzyme hydrolyzes CoA, preferring oxidized CoA disulfide. 3. It may detoxify oxidized CoA species or help regulate peroxisomal CoA/acyl-CoA levels.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; enzyme assay; substrate specificity analysis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes CoA degradation/turnover rather than synthesis

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null mice have defective stress response in tissues normally expressing Vanin-1
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modulating oxidative stress response and pantetheine salvage, but not direct CoA synthesis enhancement
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cysteamine acts as an antioxidant; pantothenate can re-enter CoA metabolism
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated by oxidative stress; highly expressed in embryonic gonad and select tissues
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Vanin-1 encodes a pantetheinase that degrades pantetheine to pantothenate and cysteamine. 2. Vanin-1 is induced by oxidative stress. 3. Vanin-1 null mice have defective tissue stress responses, implicating this CoA-derived pathway in antioxidant defense.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Null mutant analysis; expression studies; biochemical pathway assignment
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a CoA degradation/salvage-linked enzyme family rather than core biosynthetic step
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes conversion of pantetheine to pantothenate and antioxidant cysteamine
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Embryonic gonad and oxidative stress-responsive tissues

Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion causes ~15-fold reduction in mitochondrial CoA, decreases mitochondrial CoA-dependent enzyme activities, and produces petite phenotype with poor growth on nonfermentable carbon sources
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to redistribute CoA into mitochondria, but severe mitochondrial growth defects caution against perturbation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Required for mitochondrial CoA availability supporting oxidative metabolism
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LEU5 encodes a mitochondrial carrier required for CoA accumulation in the matrix. 2. LEU5 deletion reduces mitochondrial CoA about 15-fold without affecting cytosolic CoA. 3. Reduced mitochondrial CoA lowers activities of CoA-dependent enzymes and causes a petite growth phenotype.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion; mitochondrial CoA measurements; enzyme activity and growth phenotyping
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Supports transport of CoA from cytosol to mitochondria rather than in situ synthesis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes mitochondrial CoA accumulation and oxidative metabolic competence

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low abundance in E. coli due to weak promoter and low-usage codons
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed step; primary rate-limiting and feedback-regulated control point
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong metabolic engineering target to increase CoA, but feedback-insensitive variants or precursor supplementation are needed; overexpression alone gives limited gains due to feedback
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes CoA synthesis from pantothenate; wild-type enzyme limits CoA accumulation through feedback inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. coaA encodes pantothenate kinase catalyzing the first committed step from pantothenate to 4'-phosphopantothenate. 2. E. coli CoaA is feedback-inhibited by CoA and less strongly by CoA thioesters, making it the primary control point of CoA biosynthesis. 3. Overexpression raises CoA only modestly, whereas a feedback-resistant CoaA[R106A] increases CoA production in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Feedback-resistant or overexpressed coaA can increase acetate overflow; unregulated synthesis may deplete pantothenate supply
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: ATP-dependent phosphorylation of pantothenate to form 4'-phosphopantothenate
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native coaA promoter in E. coli is weak; multicopy plasmid overexpression used experimentally
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bacterial type I PanK/CoaA; A-type ATP-binding motif GX4GKS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Lys101 is essential for ATP and CoA binding; K101M abolishes activity; R106A reduces feedback inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Carbon source shifts such as glucose versus acetate alter CoA species and ATP levels, changing feedback inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; purification; enzyme kinetics; overexpression; site-directed mutagenesis; crystal structures; in vivo CoA measurements
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the first committed precursor toward CoA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Universal acyl-group carrier essential for central metabolism

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Alternative CoA biosynthetic entry step in some bacteria
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antimicrobial target in bacteria lacking canonical coaA, but biochemical characterization is still needed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely complements pantothenate kinase deficiency and supports CoA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. coaX was reported to complement the E. coli coaA15(Ts) mutant. 2. Homologs occur in bacteria lacking recognizable coaA genes. 3. It likely encodes a distinct bacterial pantothenate kinase, but biochemical proof is pending.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Distinct bacterial pantothenate kinase class, unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned from a patent report; evidence is indirect via complementation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed step; key regulated step in fungi
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for tuning fungal CoA metabolism, but inhibition by acetyl-CoA/palmitoyl-CoA must be considered
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes CoA synthesis; activity is potently inhibited by acetyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A. nidulans panK encodes the first eukaryotic pantothenate kinase identified. 2. The enzyme is unrelated to bacterial CoaA but homologous to mammalian/fungal PanKs. 3. It is selectively and potently inhibited by acetyl-CoA, indicating distinct eukaryotic regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Feedback inhibition by acyl-CoAs may constrain pathway engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cloned by complementation of E. coli coaA15(Ts); ATP-binding site differs from bacterial type A/B motifs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional complementation; in vitro enzyme assay; kinetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: CoA biosynthetic entry step in plants
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Likely committed entry step; regulatory status little known
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modifying chloroplast CoA supply and lipid metabolism in plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports plant CoA biosynthesis; chloroplast-localized PanK likely supplies CoA for fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis AtCoaA encodes a plant pantothenate kinase homologous to fungal and human PanKs. 2. Plant PanK activity is localized to chloroplasts, where CoA demand is high. 3. AtCoaA activity was verified in vitro, but its detailed regulation remains unclear.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Spinach leaf PanK is inhibited by malonyl-CoA rather than CoA/acetyl-CoA; may reflect plant-specific regulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sequence homology; in vitro activity assay
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports de novo fatty acid synthesis and lipid metabolism

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Human liver and kidney highest; lower in heart and skeletal muscle; mouse PanK1a high in heart and kidney, PanK1b in liver and kidney
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: CoA biosynthetic entry step in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Liver, kidney, heart depending on isoform and species
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for elevating mammalian CoA; isoform-specific control and precursor supply must be considered
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression of PanK1b markedly increases intracellular CoA; isoforms differ in feedback regulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PANK1 encodes mammalian PanK1 isoforms that catalyze the first committed step in CoA synthesis. 2. PanK1 isoforms are differentially expressed and differentially regulated by CoA species. 3. Enforced overexpression of mPanK1b causes a 13-fold increase in CoA, showing PanK expression can strongly control CoA levels.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess CoA may alter acyl-CoA pools and downstream lipid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: PANK1a promoter contains putative carbohydrate response, SREBP, and glucose response elements
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Eukaryotic PanK family; PanK1a contains N-terminal regulatory extension
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PanK1b is stimulated by CoA and weakly inhibited by acetyl-CoA; PanK1a is inhibited by CoA, acetyl-CoA, malonyl-CoA and long-chain acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fasting, feeding, diabetes, hypolipidemic drugs, insulin, glucagon, glucocorticoids, exogenous pantothenate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous overexpression; in vitro enzyme assays; tissue expression analysis; cellular CoA measurement
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Mammalian cytosol and tissues such as liver
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central acyl-transfer cofactor in oxidative and lipid metabolism

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: CoA biosynthetic entry step in mitochondria-associated compartment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Most tissues; highest in liver reported
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Primary rate-limiting PanK isoform in its compartment
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important biomedical target due to NBIA disease linkage; mitochondrial sensitivity to acyl-CoAs is a key constraint
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes CoA pathway entry in a mitochondria-associated context and is highly sensitive to acyl-CoA inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PANK2 encodes a mitochondrial PanK isoform processed into smaller active proteins. 2. Human PanK2 is strongly inhibited by long-chain acyl-CoA, acetyl-CoA and malonyl-CoA. 3. Mutations in PANK2 are linked to neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disease-associated mutations impair neurological function; high sensitivity may suppress activity under many metabolic states
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Eukaryotic PanK family with mitochondrial targeting sequence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role in intramitochondrial CoA metabolism remains unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene mapping; cDNA expression; mitochondrial processing/localization; enzyme assays; mutation analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest in liver, followed by muscle and heart
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: CoA biosynthetic entry step in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the primary rate-limiting PanK family; specific control not characterized here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tissue-specific lever for CoA modulation, but regulatory properties were not characterized in this review
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Predicted to support CoA synthesis in tissues with high expression
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PANK3 is one of the mammalian PanK genes. 2. Its transcript is highest in liver, followed by muscle and heart. 3. Feedback properties were not yet characterized in the review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms first committed precursor toward CoA

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Putative CoA biosynthetic entry step in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Currently uncertain because PanK activity itself is questioned
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potentially contributes to CoA metabolism, but enzymatic assignment is uncertain
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PANK4 is a homologous mammalian gene mapped on chromosome 1. 2. It is expressed most highly in muscle and heart. 3. Its protein is divergent and may not be a bona fide PanK.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Divergent PanK-like protein with extended C-terminus
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review explicitly raises possibility that PANK4 may not be a PanK

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not primary; essential mid-pathway bifunctional step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising antibacterial target because prokaryotic bifunctional architecture differs from eukaryotes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts 4'-phosphopantothenate to 4'-phosphopantetheine through PPCS and PPCDC activities, enabling CoA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. coaBC encodes the bifunctional FMN-containing enzyme carrying both PPCS (CoaB) and PPCDC (CoaC) activities in E. coli. 2. CoaB uses CTP to ligate cysteine to 4'-phosphopantothenate. 3. CoaC decarboxylates 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine through an FMN-dependent mechanism to generate 4'-phosphopantetheine.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sequentially forms 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine from 4'-phosphopantothenate plus cysteine, then decarboxylates it to 4'-phosphopantetheine
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional enzyme with CoaB synthetase domain and CoaC HFCD flavodecarboxylase domain
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Streptococci and enterococci often separate CoaB and CoaC into distinct proteins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene renaming/identification; purification; domain dissection; mutagenesis; enzymology
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for PPAT step

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mid-pathway ligation step in plant CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to enhance plant CoA synthesis; ATP dependence aligns it with eukaryotic PPCS enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes ATP-dependent PPCS reaction in plant CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCoaB is a plant homolog of human monofunctional PPCS. 2. Its enzymatic activity was confirmed in vitro. 3. Unlike bacterial CoaB, it uses ATP rather than CTP.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: AtCoaB* paralog may be nonfunctional, complicating annotation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates 4'-phosphopantothenate and ligates cysteine to form 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AtCoaB* shares high identity but its function is unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sequence homology; in vitro enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct precursor of PPCDC step

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mid-pathway decarboxylation step in plant CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to support plant CoA production and stress-related metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes PPC decarboxylation to generate 4'-phosphopantetheine in plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtHAL3a/AtCoaC1 is a functional plant PPC decarboxylase. 2. It belongs to the HFCD flavoprotein family and forms a trimer. 3. His90 and Cys175 are critical active-site residues and the reaction proceeds through an oxidatively decarboxylated aminoethenethiol intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Also linked to plant growth and salt tolerance, suggesting pleiotropy
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: FMN-dependent decarboxylation of 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine to 4'-phosphopantetheine via aminoethenethiol intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AtHAL3b lacks direct evidence for the same activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assay; mutagenesis; structural analysis of mutant complex
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for PPAT step

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mid-pathway ligation step in mammalian CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful to reconstruct or complement CoA pathway; less attractive than PanK for flux control
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes ATP-preferred PPCS reaction that supports human CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Human PPCS is encoded by a single-copy gene and catalyzes only the synthetase step. 2. The cloned cDNA complements loss of the essential bacterial enzyme in E. coli. 3. Human PPCS uses ATP about four times more efficiently than CTP and forms a dimer.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates 4'-phosphopantothenate and ligates cysteine to form 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Structure suggests ping-pong mechanism via acyladenylate intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; E. coli complementation; protein purification; in vitro assay; structural analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct precursor for PPCDC step

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mid-pathway decarboxylation step in mammalian CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for pathway reconstruction; less direct for controlling flux than PanK
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes PPC decarboxylation in human CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Human PPCDC is encoded by a single-copy gene distinct from PPCS. 2. The cDNA complements the corresponding essential bacterial function in E. coli. 3. The enzyme is a trimeric FMN-binding protein closely related to plant AtHAL3a.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; E. coli complementation; protein purification; in vitro assay; structural analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for PPAT step

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Secondary regulatory step, especially when PanK control is bypassed
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Antibacterial drug target due to divergence from mammalian enzyme and structural information; may become bottleneck when PanK is overexpressed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes adenylylation of 4'-phosphopantetheine to dephospho-CoA; likely feedback-regulated by CoA and can restrict pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. coaD encodes PPAT catalyzing ATP-dependent adenylylation of 4'-phosphopantetheine. 2. The enzyme is a hexameric allosteric nucleotidyltransferase with half-of-sites reactivity. 3. CoaD represents a secondary control point because CoA binding and 4'-phosphopantetheine accumulation indicate feedback regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: When upstream PanK is overexpressed or derepressed, flux limitation shifts to CoaD and 4'-phosphopantetheine accumulates/exits cells
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the adenylyl group of ATP to 4'-phosphopantetheine to form dephospho-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Nucleotidyltransferase alpha/beta phosphodiesterase superfamily; HXGH motif
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Human PPAT is part of bifunctional CoA synthase and is sequence-divergent from bacterial CoaD
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: CoA feedback status; upstream CoaA overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; purification; kinetic and structural analysis; metabolite accumulation studies
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of final CoA

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal phosphorylation step in bacterial CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as primary control step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antibacterial target; final step enzyme with conserved kinase fold
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes the final phosphorylation of dephospho-CoA to CoA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. coaE encodes bacterial dephospho-CoA kinase, the final enzyme of the pathway. 2. It phosphorylates the 3'-hydroxyl of dephospho-CoA using ATP. 3. The enzyme has a P-loop kinase fold and conformationally mobile substrate/lid domains important for catalysis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers phosphate from ATP to the 3'-hydroxyl of dephospho-CoA to produce CoA
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: P-loop NTP-binding kinase; Walker motif GX4GKT/S
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: E. coli enzyme can trimerize in sulfate; H. influenzae homolog is monomeric but structurally similar
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BLAST-based identification; purification; enzyme assay; crystal structure

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late CoA biosynthesis adenylylation step in plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for plant CoA engineering; substrate specificity suggests precise pathway fidelity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes PPAT step using 4'-phosphopantetheine, supporting plant CoA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCoaD was identified by homology to human bifunctional CoA synthase PPAT domain. 2. Its activity was confirmed in vitro. 3. It is specific for 4'-phosphopantetheine and does not accept 4'-phosphopantothenoylcysteine.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers adenylyl group from ATP to 4'-phosphopantetheine to form dephospho-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plant PPAT, more similar to human than bacterial counterpart
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Low similarity to E. coli monofunctional CoaD

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal phosphorylation step in plant CoA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to complete plant pathway engineering modules
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes the final phosphorylation step to generate CoA in plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCoaE is the Arabidopsis homolog of dephospho-CoA kinase. 2. It is more similar to bacterial CoaE than to the human DPCK domain. 3. Its activity was confirmed in vitro.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late two-step CoA synthase module in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important target for modulating mammalian CoA synthesis and a contrasting non-bacterial architecture for antimicrobial selectivity
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Homo sapiens; Mus musculus; Sus scrofa
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes conversion of 4'-phosphopantetheine to CoA via dephospho-CoA intermediate; late step can pull pathway to completion in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Mammalian CoA synthase is a bifunctional PPAT/DPCK enzyme encoded on chromosome 17 in humans. 2. The mouse homolog localizes to the cytoplasmic aspect of the outer mitochondrial membrane. 3. The coupled terminal phosphorylation step makes the overall late pathway effectively irreversible toward CoA formation in mammals.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Activity may be modulated by membrane phospholipids; possible feedback inhibition by CoA or CoA thioesters is suggested but not directly tested
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: First adenylylates 4'-phosphopantetheine to dephospho-CoA, then phosphorylates dephospho-CoA to CoA
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional PPAT/DPCK enzyme; PPAT region contains HXXH motif and Rossmann-like fold
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A carboxy-terminal truncation retains only PPAT activity; phosphatidylcholine and phosphatidylethanolamine stimulate activity
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Homo sapiens; Mus musculus; Sus scrofa
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; cloning from porcine peptides; heterologous expression; localization; enzyme assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product through terminal module
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cytoplasmic side of mitochondrial outer membrane-associated CoA pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central cofactor for energy and lipid metabolism

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Increased CoA supports beta-oxidation and processing of CoA thioesters
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates hPANK1alpha transcription, increasing PanK activity and CoA production under fibrate treatment
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: CoA biosynthesis via transcriptional control of pantothenate kinase expression
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: hPANK1a promoter; inducible by bezafibrate/PPARalpha pathway
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward nutrient/drug-responsive activation to increase CoA supply for fatty acid oxidation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPARalpha mediates transcriptional regulation of human PANK1alpha. 2. Bezafibrate activates PPARalpha and selectively increases hPANK1alpha transcript, protein, and enzyme activity in HepG2 cells. 3. CoA and 4'-phosphopantetheine levels rise in parallel, linking transcriptional control to CoA pool expansion.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PANK1a promoter also contains putative carbohydrate response, SREBP, and glucose response elements
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could broadly reprogram lipid metabolism because PPARalpha controls many beta-oxidation genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Agonist treatment; promoter analysis; transcript/protein/activity/metabolite measurements
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha; nuclear receptor
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Bezafibrate; hypolipidemic fibrates; lipid metabolic state
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions as transcriptional nuclear receptor complex, exact partner not specified in text
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Biomedically relevant for pharmacological elevation of CoA in liver; not a plant breeding target
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent transcriptional regulator that raises CoA production capacity in response to fibrate treatment
